records.ety: [OE. departen to divide, part, depart, F. dpartir to divide, distribute, se dpartir to separate one's self, depart; pref. d- (L. de) + partir to part, depart, fr. L. partire, partiri, to divide, fr. pars part. See Part.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Departed; p. pr. & vb. n. Departing.]

defs: To part; to divide; to separate.

To go forth or away; to quit, leave, or separate, as from a place or a person; to withdraw; -- opposed to arrive; -- often with from before the place, person, or thing left, and for or to before the destination.
quotes: I will depart to mine own land.
He which hath no stomach to this fight, Let him depart.

defs: To forsake; to abandon; to desist or deviate (from); not to adhere to; -- with from; as, we can not depart from our rules; to depart from a title or defense in legal pleading.
quotes: If the plan of the convention be found to depart from republican principles.
The glory is departed from Israel.

defs: To quit this world; to die.
quotes: Lord, now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace.

defs: To part thoroughly; to dispart; to divide; to separate.
quotes: Till death departed them, this life they lead.

defs: To divide in order to share; to apportion.
quotes: And here is gold, and that full great plentee, That shall departed been among us three.

defs: Division; separation, as of compound substances into their ingredients.
quotes: The chymists have a liquor called water of depart.

defs: A going away; departure; hence, death.
